reasoning: Reaching 15 billion cubic meters (bcm) of natural gas imports from Russia to the EU in a single quarter is highly mathematically improbable for 2025. In 2023, the total annual Russian gas imports to the EU were around 43 bcm, meaning the quarterly average was under 11 bcm. Throughout 2024, pipeline deliveries and LNG shipments remained well below the 15 bcm/quarter threshold. With Russia officially ending pipeline deliveries through Ukraine in January 2025 (which previously contributed ~15 bcm/year, or ~3.75 bcm/quarter), the EU's direct pipeline imports of Russian gas are now essentially limited to TurkStream (~4 bcm/quarter to Europe). Even if Russian LNG shipments max out (typically ~4-5 bcm/quarter), the total sum falls far below 15 bcm, likely struggling to exceed 9-10 bcm per quarter. Thus, without the restoration of the Ukraine transit route or Nord Stream, there is virtually no logistical capacity or political will to import 15 bcm of Russian gas in any quarter of 2025. The freeze value of ~0.01 reflects this near-0 probability clearly.
